comments.title: Many worlds theory
comments.content:
JMG -

Would you suppose there's any metaphysical validity to the "many worlds" theory, e.g., there's a reality in which the South won the Civil War, etc.? I don't know enough about quantum mechanics to venture an opinion, but even if I did, I think I'd rely on my reflective intuition to give me an answer, and as of now my intuition tells me no, there is no parallel reality in which the South won the Civil War or that the nazis triumphed or that your hot chocolate-quaffing shoggoths really exist.

Still, the many-worlds theory does kind of resonate with the idea that within God there is infinite potentiality waiting to be actualized, and that Plato said something to the effect of "God has a moral obligation to actualize all potentiality". The many-worlds theory would certainly seem to fit that bill well enough. But I'm thinking that we and all self-aware creatures serve in the actualization of infinite potentiality by acts of the imagination. No, those hot chocolate fans, the shoggoths do not exist in a material, parallel earth, but - by virtue of your imagination, and Mr. Lovecraft's, they do exist in the upper planes as archetypes - and are becoming more actualized by the day, I would think - and so in this manner, infinite potentially really is being actualized by the imaginative acts of countless creatures who themselves are actualizations of imaginative acts.

Have to say, I would have loved to know how Dion Fortune would have interpreted the many-worlds concept.

Thanks - Will M

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content:
I know of no reason to take the "many worlds" theory as more than a neat gimmick for science fiction -- I enjoy H. Beam Piper's Lord Kalvan of Otherwhen as much as the next geek, but I don't take it any more seriously than I take shoggoths sipping hot chocolate.

Do you have any idea where you heard or read that Plato said that somewhere? I don't claim to be an expert on his thought, but that doesn't sound like him at all. Classical philosophy in general is refreshingly free of the language of moral obligation.

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content:
It's been a while, I'd have to hunt it down. It's possible that I'm misremembering the "moral obligation" - it might have been something more akin to "logical consequence of", etc.

Anyway, my larger point was that the many-worlds theory, like the proclamation of some that it's our destiny to colonize the galaxy, is really an overly-concrete and materialistic manifestation of a spiritual yearning.

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content: If I may channel Oswald Spengler for a moment, it's a kind of yearning that's specific to Faustian (aka Western) culture, the endless outward rush into the infinite. That sort of thing makes sense to us -- which is of course no guarantee that it makes any kind of sense to anyone else.

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content:
Okay, I'm pretty sure Will is thinking of metaphysical necessity, rather than moral obligation. I have recently been told that in Aristotle, at least, we find the claim that there are no unactualized potentialies. Alas, I'm still waiting on my informant to provide the citation.

On the one hand, this claim could mean that given infinite time (which we have in a beginningless classical cosmos) every genuine potential which eventually be actualized. On the other hand, reading the claim the other way around, then from the point of view of eternity (from which God can see all of time "at once"), then any apparent capacity which never turns out to get actualized is not, strictly speaking, a potentiality.

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content: Fair enough. In that latter case, though, "potentiality" means only "an actuality that we don't see yet," or something close to that.

replies.title: Re: Many worlds theory
replies.content:
I do have to admit though that reading your description of Yog-Sothoth consisting of "floating luminous spheres that reflect the entire universe" while simultaneously spanning all space and time... caused the many-worlds images of the bubbles of universes floating around hyperspace to pop into my head...

I guess that direction of thought would be incorrect though if Yog-Sothoth (or our version there-of) is confined to spanning all of space and time contained within our particular Universe. Was an enjoyable train of thought none-the-less.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You always need to check transits against your natal chart, or the natal or foundation chart of the person or thing in question. A Sun trine Jupiter in the sky is great, but if it doesn't relate to your natal placements, somebody else gets the benefit. Mind you, if there's a Sun trine Jupiter in the sky and (say) Jupiter is applying to conjunction with your natal Moon, you don't just get the Jupiter conjunction, you get a conjunction with a Jupiter that's being benefited by a trine with the Sun, which is even better. (You probably also get Sun trine natal Moon, which is also great.

2) One could indeed. It would be fairly easy, for example, to go through the various titles of Aphrodite and fit them to the Tree, with Aphrodite Urania as Kether, Aphrodite Pandemos as Malkuth, and the others fitting in between.

3) Buddhist deities all get their jobs via that kind of process, and so do the deities in some other traditions. I'm not sure how far you can generalize from that, but it's at least worth exploring.

comments.title: Which magic system
comments.content: How does one choose which system of magic to study?

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content: The same way you choose what kind of career you want to have, who you want to marry, etc. You check out the available options and make a choice!

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content: I guess should have been a little more specific.  For example you have three sets of magical systems in your books, Golden dawn, Druid magic and hybrid of the two.  What kind of people gravitate to each of these?   What are the differences between them.  You all ways say do one stystem completely and don’t mix.  I am just wondering what I should look at to choose.

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content:
The standard Golden Dawn system works with Judeo-Christian symbols and entities, and is best suited to people who don't have a problem getting along with Jehovah. The Celtic Golden Dawn system is best suited for people who want systematic training in ceremonial magic and are okay working with the (mostly Welsh) deities reverenced in the Druid Revival tradition. The Druidry Handbook and its sequels were originally meant for initiates of the Ancient Order of Druids in America (AODA), and has found a somewhat broader audience among people who are into nature spirituality and want a magical and spiritual system compatible with that.

It's important, though, not to try to make a choice of a system to follow with your thinking mind alone. I'd encourage you to consider reading the introductory books in each sequence, and see which of them resonates best with you on all levels.

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content: Thanks John Michael.  That gives me the outline I was looking for.  Learning that magic is real in the middle of my life has really been a joy

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content: I get that. When I was going through the worst part of my life, my middle school years, knowing that magic is real was most of what kept me from walking out in front of a truck.

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content:
Piggybacking on the previous question: What are the advantages/disadvantages to studying magic with an established school (i.e. through correspondence course) or on one's own (i.e. by reading and working through your books)?

Thanks for the FAQ page, by the way. I had a look through and learned a lot! I appreciate your patience with all the questions. I'm also looking forward to the release of Ascendant.

- Dylan

replies.title: Re: Which magic system
replies.content:
It depends entirely on how you personally learn best. If you like learning in a social context and prefer to have lots of advice from experienced people, by all means join an existing school. If social interactions are challenging for you and you'd be happier looking up the answers yourself, do it on your own.

In my experience, though, learning at least a little on your own before you go looking for a school is a very useful habit. It helps you avoid the kind of "magical school" that's purely there to bolster somebody's ego or make a profit off you.

comments.title: Coincidence?
comments.content:
Ok, here's a weird one. Just for context: I ran across the "Archdruid Report" for the first time last year, read the whole thing back-to-back, a lot of your other books as well and ended up dabbling in the occult a bit as a result. However, I haven't gotten much beyond the LRP and some meditation once in while (in my defence, we have 3 small children).

Now, a while ago I was looking out the kitchen window and was struck by the realization that there were five (otherwise differing) _white_ cars standing next to each other outside. The pure randomness of it was one thing but I was surprised at how forceful the realization was, like someone else was jabbing my nose at it.

Then, a few days later, I was on the balcony, glanced into the street around the corner and there were again five (different) white cars parked in a row.

It hasn't let me go since and I'm not sure why. I'm not usually one to pay much attention to parked cars. The forcefulness of the realization the first time it happened still resonates.

Do you have any ideas on how to figure out if there's a meaning there? If there is even anything to make of, my old self would have dismissed it as entirely random - my current self is not so sure.

replies.title: Re: Coincidence?
replies.content: Meditate on the image of five white cars in a row and see if you can figure out what it means to you. It's as though you dreamed of five white cars -- and of course, in a certain sense, that's what happened... ;-)

replies.title: Re: Voor and stage presence/charisma
replies.content: I don't know of anything that covers the training process. I do know that it's not necessarily safe -- one of the reasons that so many musicians, fundamentalist preachers, etc. go hypersexual and then burn out is that way too much life energy (okay, we can call it voor) flows into them during their performances and seeks any outlet that happens to be available. Austin Miles' fascinating memoir Don't Call Me Brother is a very useful source here; Miles was a circus ringmaster who got caught up in the Evangelical frenzy of the late 1970s and early 1980s, became a preacher for a while, and got to see the seamy inside of the Bakkers' religious empire.

comments.title: Visitation or Exhaustion?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Here comes a magic-medical question... In the previous 7 years, I have undergone and completed several magical training courses, including your LRM and Druid Magic, and I‘m still very much at it. At some point, however, I have developed fits of insomnia (after having slept perfectly all my life) that can‘t be reduced to my daily life (which didn‘t change outwardly) nor to an ordinary medical condition (I‘ve checked).

The symptoms, which occur nearly always after emotionally charged encounters, usually (but not always) with women (I‘m a straight and rather conventionally married male, 36, magnetic but introverted), consist of restlessness, a sudden wakefulness that begins around midnight, and—most importantly—cold arms; and this coldness is not merely thermal in nature: It doesn‘t yield to a few dozen knuckle push-ups, for instance. The latter symptom is so essential that it serves me as a handle for insomnia in my journal (January 23: Usual practice; night, cold arms).

So—is this a visitation, a parasite, an attack, emotional exhaustion, or what? Naturally, I attempted purifying baths, banishing, and the cutting of cords (in any combination), but to no avail. Etheric shielding seems to alleviate the situation somewhat, but on most occasions I‘m reduced to the mundan medication of a large bowl of cereals, a detective story, and waiting for an hour or three.

Any insights are highly appreciated!

Thanks,
VG

replies.title: Re: Visitation or Exhaustion?
replies.content:
I will provide a bit of personal experience.

I sleep with the window in my sleeping room open. I too often wake up in the middle of the night, apparently for no reason, many times from an intense dream. And when I look through the window, in the top left corner, there is an astrological planet there. More often it is Jupiter, or Mars, some times the Moon. Many times there is a planet in transit (at the highest point of the sky) at the moment.

I suggest you to try to gather information on the position of the planets if you suddenly wake up during the night; like me, you might be sensitive to planet energies.

No amount of other people telling me this would make me believe it during my angry atheist years; now I just take it as a fact of life--it would be absurd to ignore hard evidence like this.

replies.title: Re: Visitation or Exhaustion?
replies.content: If it's legal in the jurisdiction where you live, you might consider consulting with a licensed provider of some kind of alternative medicine. That's not something I've encountered, and in your place I'd probably pay a visit to a good acupuncturist.

In my view, the outcome will depend almost entirely on the mages involved, not on the forms of magic they use.

Magic comes in many forms, and some of these forms rest on such radically different foundations that it is literally impossible for one and the same human being to practice all of them even minimally.

Additionally, each variety of magic seems to call its mages to itself. Mages don't chose their magics, not if they want to be really effective; magics choose their mages. (And some people are never chosen to be mages by any form of magic.)

It has actually seemed to me to be very like a process of human mutual courtship. Mage and magic need to circle around one another in desire and see how they work together. The "chemistry" between the two has to feel right for the relationship to flourish.

Never think that any magic is a lifeless system of thought and practice, comparable to, say, geometry or logic or physics or politics or even ethics, which anyoe might master. Magic, taken as a whole, is (IMHO) best understood as a community of living immaterial beings, who (unlike humans) are not wholly distinct from one another, but have interpenetrating, amorphous, fluid identities.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch Tradition
comments.content:
I have five-ish questions regarding the tradition that includes the Dolmen Arch, Druid Magic Handbook, Druidry Handbook, and Gnostic Celtic Church:

First, what is the name of said tradition? I've been calling it the "Dolmen Arch Tradition," but I'm wondering if it has another name.

Second, I'd like to learn as much as I can about the tradition's history. I'm aware of the AODA's history page on their website, and of the introduction in the GCC and DA correspondence course, but am looking for more.

Third, is The Way of the Cauldrons part of the same tradition, and are there any other so far unreleaed parts of the tradition that you know of?

I suppose questions 4 and 5 are also asking if there are general guidelines for approaching parts of a tradition after having completed other parts.

I feel very at home in this tradition, and am very grateful to you for reconstructing it and revitalizing it. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Tradition
replies.content:
1) It doesn't really have a name, other than "the traditional lore of AODA and its related orders, along with a bunch of stuff that I developed from that." You can certainly call it "the Dolmen Arch tradition" if you like.

2) What's in those sources is basically everything I've been able to find. I didn't find definite documentation for the existence of Dr. Juliet Ashley, the third Grand Archdruid of AODA and a crucial figure in the emergence of the lore we've got, until a few years ago -- and then it was a couple of paragraphs in an obscure New Age volume from the 1960s. If you'd like to take up the quest for more, by all means, but it may take you quite a bit of work.

3) The Way of the Cauldrons comes from one of the other things Dr. Ashley was into, a system of psychological and emotional alchemy. When I have time to complete that and make it available, it'll be compatible with the Dolmen Arch tradition, but it's going to take some changes to make that happen.

4 and 5) Once you've completed the Dolmen Arch, you'll have a sufficiently deep and extensive grasp of the tradition that you can pick and choose from the other books -- so, no, you don't have to go back to square 1.

This was the form of magic that developed in the New Thought movement, essentially "mental magic" (as it is often called). No spirits or any other sort of beings were involved in this sort of magic, just methods for shaping your own self and your own life to suit your needs as you grew up.

comments.title: Orphic Hymns
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. Many thanks for the weekly sharing of your wisdom and both practical and academic knowledge via dreamwidth!

I am considering the taking up a practice of reciting all the Orphic Hymns to the planets daily, as I thought that it would help me be attuned to the planets in a “balanced” way. I have been reciting something similar, but in Sanskrit as per the Hindu tradition (the Navagraha Stotra, which briefly praises the seven classical planets in the same order as the days of the week, with the two lunar nodes tagged onto the end). But I figured that since the Western and Hindu occult traditions are far from identical, I thought that it might be best to ask about this before impetuously trying it out.

So, my questions are: (1) is the daily recitation of all the Orphic Hymns an accepted practice among Western astrologers or geomancers; (2) if not, is there any particular reason why; and (3) if it is an accepted practice, is there a particular order of hymns that should be followed?

With best regards,

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Orphic Hymns
replies.content:
The classic practice -- and I know people who do this, with great benefit -- is to recite one planetary Orphic hymn per day, using the relationship of days to planets:

Sunday: Sun
Monday: Moon
Tuesday: Mars
Wednesday: Mercury
Thursday: Jupiter
Friday: Venus
Saturday: Saturn

If you really want to be traditional, burn some incense to the planetary deity while you recite the Hymn, and then follow with an extempore prayer. Done regularly, this is a potent practice that will have significant positive results in your life.

Reciting all the Orphic Hymns would keep you busy for a while, as there are 86 of them! (You can find them here, in case you don't have them all.) If there were Orphic monasteries, I could see the monks dividing them into cycles and chanting all of them over the course of a week or something, as a litany, but all in one day might be a bit much.

I also did some research and found many websites talking about re-purposing sexual energy and one relating it to kundalini energy.

replies.title: Re: The repurposing of sexual energy
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, there's nothing wrong with sex. Sexuality is natural, normal, and healthy for most people, and if your vital energy wants to express itself in the time-honored way and you can arrange to do that in a context where everyone involved is freely consenting, that's probably your best option.

Second, kundalini is one extremely specialized way of working with the life force. It's also extremely dangerous unless you have a competent teacher. Uncontrolled unbalanced kundalini release can kill you stone cold dead. I strongly encourage you not to get into anything related to kundalini practice unless you can do it under the personal supervision of somebody who's good at it.

Third, it's a common bad habit to try to repress a natural drive by the (mis)use of the will. That's self-defeating, as it gets you locked into an internal battle that neither side will win. If you want to work with the life force, your best bet is to get to work on some system of inner practice -- for example, the material in The Druid Magic Handbook -- and let the energy respond to the shift in the way it's used.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The core primary emotions are desire and fear. Neither of them is "bad," and each can manifest in more or less balanced and ripened form. Love is a balanced and ripened form of desire -- note that "desire" emphatically does not only mean sexual desire! Anything you want, long for, hope for, delight in being in the presence of, is an object of some form of desire, just as anything you respect, distrust, are uncomfortable around, or hate is an object of some form of fear. Yes, you can also feel desire and fear for the same thing!

Fear and desire can both be problematic when they haven't had the chance to become balanced and ripe -- when, for example, a childhood terror never gets the acknowledgment and comfort that would allow it to be released, or when an unadmitted desire festers in the dark corners of the mind. You can also get unhelpful fusions of the two, such as greed -- as you've noted, that's desire poisoned by fear.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. The thing I'd ask in response is whether other traditions have had a similar upsurge, or is it just Catholics who are having more trouble with demons these days?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In my observation, other Christian traditions have also been dealing with "demons" much more. In my opinionated observation, this issue is partly the surrounding world, and partly changes within Christian practice. Traditional liturgical Christian ritual is done by carefully trained people following strict person disciplines [1], in spaces with layers and layers of protection[2], following forms that have hundreds of years of history. With the changing emphases following the Second Vatican Council (affecting all the liturgical churches), and the growth of the "spiritual warfare" movement in some corners of the Evangelical movement, you had much more invocation, and attempted banishing, being done by people with little discipline, and little training, and no protections. Even with no real knowledge, those people scared me as a young man and I still think their practices are dangerous to them and everyone around them.

So yes, there's much more observation of "demonic activity" across Christian faiths, but the strongly traditional forms seem to have been largely immune and to be where people are looking for help that actually helps.

1) Personal disciplines: not primarily the avoidance of sex expected of Roman Catholic clerics, but the requirement to pray and meditate on the Psalms every day, to fast in particular ways at particular times, and so on.

2) Layers and layers of protection: these gave me my first TSW moment (a story for elsewhere and elsewhen.) A traditional church, like the Episcopal church that was the first liturgical church I attended, has ceremonial doors in consecrated walls, an entry area, a second set of ceremonial doors, a step, a platform, a step, a higher platform, a step to the altar platform, and a metal or stone rail around the altar--each layer of the five blessed, when the church is consecrated, with its own set of formal prayers.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Nyarlathotep
replies.content:
Nyarlathotep in the original Cthulhu mythos is a strange, shadowy presence -- as with so much of his legendarium, Lovecraft got the name and some of the ideas in a dream, and later on turned the dream into an impressively creepy story about the end of the world.

In The Weird of Hali, Nyarlathotep is the soul and messenger of the Great Old Ones, a tall figure with the face of an Egyptian pharaoh, dressed in a long black coat and black hat, who wanders the world, doing the bidding of his masters. His image was inspired partly by the devil who, according to old blues songs, you could meet at the crossroads at midnight and bargain with; partly by messenger-gods such as Hermes; and partly by characters in some of the fantasy novels I wallowed in during my insufficiently misspent youth.

comments.title: Jealous Gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Hopefully this isn't an annoying question to you. Some I've asked in the past seem to have caused irritation, so I apologize for that. I'm not trying to call down the Archdruid's wrath, just better understand some of the things I'm studying/reading.

There was a discussion last week about the "God of Israel being a jealous god" and not allowing interaction/worship of other gods.

I'm curious about your experiences with that (since I believe I read that you continued some Golden Dawn work after you started druidry), if you are willing to share. Also, how/why things like assuming godforms are part of the Golden Dawn system despite working with Jehovah

Thanks,
Ross

replies.title: Re: Jealous Gods
replies.content:
When I worked with the classic GD system, that's what I worked with; I invoked angels and prayed to the Judeo-Christian god. It was always kind of an awkward fit, as I was never baptized and he's not a god I particularly admire, but we worked out a modus vivendi and it worked fairly well.

The first version of Druidry I practiced, the one taught by the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids (OBOD), was ecumenical and didn't involve invoking other deities. When I went further and joined AODA, though, I stopped doing classic Golden Dawn workings entirely, and didn't start GD work again until I'd worked up the rituals that went into the book _The Celtic Golden Dawn_, which invoke Pagan Celtic gods.

As for GD godforms, if you read the original papers you'll find that they didn't interpret those as actual gods -- rather, they're symbolic forms into which various energies flow. I disagree with that interpretation -- to me, the gods of Egypt are divine persons -- but that's the view of the original GD papers, which I'd encourage you to consult.

comments.title: Muses, Discernment of dreams, dry periods
comments.content:
Hello, John Michael.

Lately I've been following some very interesting discussions about the crossing over of deities or other spiritual beings between fiction and practice (or the former using fictional deities as guises) and am wondering how one might determine whether that is going on as one is creating art or fiction. Or for that matter, if it might be some deity of established/ancient religions poking through the fictional character.

My specific situation: I am working on a character for a possible story. She is a kitsune (Japanese fox spirit) who serves the Shinto kami Inari Okami, and sometimes worshiped as such by unaware humans. This has led me to do research on Inari Okami and Japanese fox lore to make whatever I write at least plausible.

I have been reading a book on Inari worship, "The Fox and the Jewel" by Karen A. Smyers. One day, two weeks ago, I wanted to either take a nap or read that book on my lunch break, as I had already eaten while working. I went to my car to take a nap. I fidgeted and felt restless. I could not sleep. That in itself wasn't interesting. Napping for short periods is hard. I either sleep for at least two hours, or don't sleep at all.

I got out of my car and sat outside and read some of the book. I stopped feeling restless. I felt like sitting outside and reading the book had been the right thing to do. I read a part where the author had gone to the priest of an Inari temple for a divination about the prospects of the study she was writing (the book I was now reading), and was told to walk outside and pay attention to the first person she saw on leaving the temple and come back for the priest to interpret the sign. (or something along those lines) She saw a man on a red motorbike. She told the priest what she had seen, and the priest told her that her study would get published, and mentioned that the red motorbike was significant because red is the color of Inari Okami.

I don't remember if I just looked up from the book or if I had put the book down to pick up my phone, but I noticed that in the parking lot in front of where I was sitting were two cars, both red. The one directly in front of me was a kind of burgundy color, the other a brighter red color. I don't even know if that would count but I'm including it here just in case.

Later that week, on the night of the 18th of January,
I dreamed a lot. Writing the whole thing would make this post even longer. I hope mentioning specific possible Inari elements from the whole doesn't mess things up too badly.

There was one point where I saw a golden shopping kiosk filled with Japanese white fox masks, statues, etc. At another point I saw a man dropping a coin into an offering box with a white ceramic fox on it. Red also appeared, in the form of who according to the dream was a drag queen in a red dress (which could relate to Inari Okami's variable gender depiction?-- man, woman, androgynous bodhisattva)

The week after that, on and off, I have been taken with drawing the fictional fox-spirit character (as distinct from Inari Okami), even at one point, at work, not being able to continue working until finished.

Any ideas or tips on how to discern this kind of thing?


I'm broadly in the Pagan area, would typically consider myself Kemetic, though I have been in a dry period most of the time for the last several years. Egyptian terms still feel closest to me. Any tips on how to get out of that dry period where it feels like your connection or awareness beyond the physical is just about gone?

Thank you,

-J

replies.title: Re: Muses, Discernment of dreams, dry periods
replies.content: It sounds to me as though Inari responded to your interest by indicating that he's available if you're interested in a relationship with a kami. There's actually a fair amount of interpenetration between Shinto and contemporary Paganism these days; there's an international Faceplant group for worshipers of Inari, and a great deal more. Whether you choose to pursue that acquaintance, though, is up to you.

comments.content: How does one calculate a fortuitous time astrologically to submit a manuscript?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Start with your own natal chart. Make sure that Mercury isn't in the signs of its detriment or fall (Pisces or Sagittarius) and that it isn't in a conjunction, square or opposition aspect with Mars or Saturn. (If it is, check to see if Jupiter is okay -- not in Gemini, Virgo or Capricorn and free of those dame aspect -- and if Jupiter's messed up, see if the Sun is free of those aspects and not in Libra or Aquarius.)

We'll assume that Mercury is free of those problems. (If not, substitute the name of the other planet for Mercury.) Find a day and a time when the Moon is applying to Mercury by trine or sextile, and when neither the Moon nor Mercury is part of a square or opposition aspect with any planet, or a conjunction with Mars or Saturn. That's your time to submit the manuscript.

comments.title: Going "backwards"
comments.content: Do you think the evolution of the soul is always unidirectional, species-wise? In other words, do you think that some animals living today have led a past life as a human? I've met at least one that seems to fit the bill. A corollary question is, are humans always the last form that a soul experiences before leaving incarnation on the material plane, or would some other Earth species fit the bill, in your view?

replies.title: Re: Going "backwards"
replies.content:
Human beings can mess up and go back to an animal form to relearn some lessons. Welsh Druid lore has it that a clairvoyant can sometimes see the animal somebody's going to become in their next life, as though it paced along behind them; that spectral animal is called a cydfil. I knew a cat once who I'm pretty sure had been human in a previous life.

As for the forms of souls ready to move beyond material incarnation, I'm pretty sure that whales and dolphins are just as sentient as we are, and therefore souls can go through those forms as their last sequence of material incarnations. I suspect there has been at least one fully sentient form available on this world, for souls ready to pass on, since sometime in the Paleozoic, though the forms have of course changed many times.

comments.title: Dumb questions?
comments.content:
Is it normal for young children to have a sort of sixth sense for the occult and actually act on the pull when they are eventually adults? That and is it bad that I do not have any intention to self initiate at all based on a gut feeling not to?

The reason why I ask the first question is because I'm currently going through my life, again, and trying to find where it all started, and the youngest I can recall, at least for myself, is around 3 or 4 years old and several different instances and ideas comming up through the years that are considered heretical to most Southern Independent Baptists and society. Thus said ideas were repressed. Fast forward to a little over two years years ago, more than a few slices of humble pie, and embarrassing experiences, and now here I am.

As dumb of a question as the second inquiry may sound, it seems that there's just a general push to do it floating around, but to me it doesnt feel right. Its as if opening a door to someone's house uninvited expecting a nice and cozy welcome rather than a dangerous game of "bird shot or buck shot?". Of course I'm no where near that finish line, I got much to learn,but the question to seek out an order and or find reliable, qualified, and verified individuals who can give hands on instruction and guidance is perhaps just as important to me as asking what my plan is ("was" at this point) after highschool. Im paying dearly for not asking those kinds of questions and working towards my goals, and I'm not intent on repeating that same kind of mistake.

So far Rosicrucianism and freemasonry stand out, but I'd be hard pressed to be admitted into any Masonic order. Which leaves me with the one option until further notice.

-Copper
